articles.title: Your First 90 Days of CrossFit: What Should You Really Expect?
articles.excerpt: If you’re looking for a quick fix, CrossFit probably isn’t for you.
articles.author: Dan
articles.body:
**If you’re looking for a quick fix, CrossFit probably isn’t for you.**

That might sound like a strange thing for a CrossFit gym owner to say, but it’s true.

We live in a world where almost everything is marketed as the fastest route to better health. Six-week challenges, dramatic transformations and “life-changing” programmes appear every January and every summer. Some people get results, but many end up back where they started because nothing really changed beyond the challenge itself.

That’s never been our approach.

At CrossFit TBB, we’re trying to build something much bigger than six weeks of motivation. We want to help people become stronger, fitter and healthier for life. We often joke about “kicking ass into our 80s”, but there’s a lot of truth behind it. Staying strong, capable and independent later in life doesn’t happen by accident. It comes from looking after yourself consistently, long before you need to.

That’s why your first 90 days matter so much. Not because you’ll have transformed your body by then, but because they’re the first steps towards building habits that can genuinely change your life.

Most people walk through our doors hoping to improve something. They might want to lose weight, build strength, improve their fitness or simply feel better in themselves. They’re all great reasons to start and, with consistency, there’s every chance you’ll achieve them.

What’s interesting is that the biggest changes during your first few months often aren’t the ones people expect.

You’ll probably find yourself sleeping a little better. You’ll have more energy during the day. Everyday tasks become easier and, before long, training starts to feel like part of your week rather than another job on the to-do list.

One of my favourite changes isn’t physical at all.

It’s the moment someone stops asking themselves if they should come to the gym after work and starts wondering what today’s workout is.

That shift from relying on motivation to building a routine is where long-term progress really begins.

CrossFit has never been about becoming exceptional at one thing. The goal is to become more capable across everything life throws at you. Strong enough to carry the shopping without thinking twice. Fit enough to play with your children without needing to sit down after five minutes. Healthy enough to give yourself the best chance of staying active and independent well into later life.

For me, that’s what fitness is really about.

After coaching hundreds of people through their first CrossFit class, I’ve realised that almost everyone starts with the same doubts.

The biggest one is usually, “I need to get fit before I start.”

In reality, most people are comparing their day one to somebody else’s year five.

The person confidently moving through today’s workout was once exactly where you are now. They didn’t know the movement names, they questioned whether they belonged and they certainly weren’t moving as confidently as they are today. The only difference is they’ve had hundreds more classes to learn, improve and build confidence.

That’s why comparing yourself to everyone else is a losing game. The only comparison that really matters is between the person who walked through the door on your first day and the person you’re becoming.

That’s where good coaching comes in.

One of the biggest strengths of CrossFit is that every workout can be adapted to the person standing in front of us. If a movement is too advanced, we’ll choose one that develops the same skill. If the weight is too heavy, we’ll reduce it. If you’re ready for more, we’ll challenge you.

The goal has never been for everyone to do exactly the same workout. The goal is for everyone to achieve the same training effect. That’s what allows someone walking into their very first class to train alongside someone who’s been doing CrossFit for ten years, with both people finishing the workout fitter than when they started.

Your first 90 days aren’t about proving how fit you are. They’re about building the habits, confidence and consistency that make long-term progress possible. Everything else tends to follow.

If there’s one thing I’d like you to take away from this article, it’s this.

Don’t judge your first 90 days by the scales, the leaderboard or the mirror. Judge them by how often you show up.

The people who achieve the biggest changes usually aren’t the most naturally talented, they’re the ones who keep turning up. They trust the process, accept that progress takes time and understand that lasting health isn’t built in six weeks, it’s built through hundreds of ordinary decisions made consistently over the years.

That’s how you keep kicking ass into your 80s.
